svg.title: Locality-sensitive hashing, index time
svg.desc: Three random hyperplanes are drawn once. Each database vector, shown for x(1), is hashed by the sign of its dot product with each plane, the sign bits form its bucket key, and the vector is appended to that bucket in table 1. Additional tables repeat this with their own planes. The stored index is L hash tables of buckets.
svg.text: Index time — offline, once per corpus
stored: L hash tables of buckets
bucket where x(1) lands — this repeats for every vector and every table
the same fixed planes will hash q at query time

svg.title: Locality-sensitive hashing, query time
svg.desc: The query is hashed by the sign of its dot product with the same three fixed hyperplanes, forming a bucket key. The key selects a bucket in table 1 containing candidate vectors x(1) and x(2); the other tables contribute more candidates. The union of bucket members is verified with exact distances.
svg.text: probed bucket — q reproduced the key x(1) and x(2) got at index time
each bit: which side of one fixed random hyperplane q falls on

svg.title: KD-tree, index time
svg.desc: All database vectors are recursively split at the median of one component per level: first x1 at the root, then x2 in each half. The stored tree consists of the median thresholds and four leaf buckets holding vectors x(1) through x(8).
svg.text: Index time — offline, once per corpus
the stored tree: median thresholds plus leaf buckets
each split: the median of one component xj over the vectors in that subtree

svg.title: KD-tree, query time
svg.desc: The query descends the stored tree of median splits to leaf B. The radius test finds the query ball crosses a split plane, forcing a backtrack visit to leaf A. Exact k-nearest-neighbor search runs over the four vectors in the two visited leaves.
svg.text: ball crosses — also visit leaf A
visited by this query — greedy descent plus backtracking
tree and thresholds come from the index built offline; in high dimensions the ball crosses nearly every split

svg.title: Inverted file, index time
svg.desc: A training sample feeds k-means, producing four centroids. Each database vector, shown for x(1), finds its nearest centroid and is appended to that cell's posting list. The stored index is one posting list per cell.
svg.text: Index time — offline, once per corpus
nearest cell for x(1) — this routing repeats for every database vector
the stored index: one posting list per cell

svg.title: Inverted file, query time
svg.desc: The query computes its distance to every centroid from the index, probes the two nearest cells, scans only those cells' posting lists containing vectors x(1) through x(4), and skips all other lists.
svg.text: scan only probed lists: x(1) … x(4)
nprobe nearest cells — the only lists touched by this query
centroids and posting lists come from the index built offline; nprobe > 1 catches near-boundary neighbors

svg.title: Product quantization, index time
svg.desc: A training sample feeds k-means, producing one codebook per subspace side by side. The vector x is split into subvectors x1 and x2; each is matched to its nearest centroid in its codebook, and the two centroid indices are stored as the code [1, 1].
svg.text: Index time — offline, once per corpus
nearest centroid per subvector xj — its index becomes one stored byte

svg.title: Product quantization, query time
svg.desc: The query is split into subvectors q1 and q2. Each subvector's distance to every centroid of its codebook is precomputed, filling two lookup table rows. The stored code of x from index time selects one entry per row, and the sum of the selected entries gives the approximate squared distance between q and x.
svg.text: table entries selected by x's code [1, 1]
table cells: distance from qj to each centroid of codebook j

svg.title: HNSW, index time — inserting one vector
svg.desc: A new vector draws a random maximum layer, then a greedy search of the existing graph finds candidate neighbors. The diversity pruning rule keeps edges to two candidates and rejects a third that is closer to an already-kept neighbor than to the new vector. The node is inserted with the kept edges.
svg.text: Index time — one insert, repeated per vector
Layer 0 — existing graph plus the new node
candidates found by the insert-time search
kept edges — stored in the graph
pruned — closer to a kept neighbor than to x(5) — keeps edge directions diverse

svg.title: HNSW hierarchical greedy search
svg.desc: Three graph layers from sparse to dense. The query enters at the top layer, greedily hops toward its nearest node, drops a layer at each local minimum, and runs a beam search on the full bottom layer, ending at the nearest neighbor.
svg.text: HNSW — greedy walk down a layered graph
nodes and edges the walk visits
upper layers: exponentially fewer nodes with long edges — layer 0: full graph, efSearch beam
Pushing the index to disk DiskANN & SPANN

svg.title: DiskANN, index time
svg.desc: Database vectors feed two build steps: a Vamana graph built with alpha pruning that keeps longer hops, and PQ compression of every vector. The stored index splits across the memory hierarchy: compact PQ codes in RAM, and one 4 KB sector per node on SSD holding the full vector and its edge list.
svg.text: Index time — offline, once per corpus
the stored index, split across the memory hierarchy
α > 1 keeps longer edges — fewer hops per query means fewer SSD reads

svg.title: SPANN, index time
svg.desc: Database vectors are clustered by k-means into many small cells. Centroids plus a tiny in-memory ANN index over them are stored in RAM. Closure assignment stores each near-boundary vector, shown for x(1), in every sufficiently near cell, so x(1) appears in both cell 1 and cell 2 posting lists on SSD.
svg.text: Index time — offline, once per corpus
closure: a boundary vector is stored in every (1+ε)-near cell — note x(1) appears twice
stored: centroids in RAM for routing, duplicated posting lists on SSD
Both plots show index build time (DiskANN left, SPANN right).
